chunk_summary: This section addresses the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children with suspected celiac disease or inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) in India, emphasizing stabilization before diagnostic or dietary interventions.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of stabilizing children with SAM before initiating diagnostic or dietary interventions.
Learn the appropriate steps for managing dehydration, hypoglycemia, and electrolyte imbalances in SAM cases.
Recognize the diagnostic considerations for celiac disease and IBD in children with SAM.
Differentiate between symptom-relief dietary restrictions and disease-defining dietary restrictions.
key_facts: Stabilization of SAM includes treating dehydration, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infection risk, followed by structured feeding with frequent small feeds.
Refeeding precautions are critical for older children with prolonged starvation or baseline electrolyte abnormalities.
For suspected celiac disease, maintain gluten exposure during initial workup to ensure tTG-IgA and biopsy results remain interpretable.
For suspected IBD, avoid prolonged dietary trials and prioritize objective labs like CBC, albumin, and CRP to document growth failure and disease severity.
Children with SAM and persistent blood in stool, edema/hypoalbuminemia, or failure to gain weight after stabilization require early referral.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Stabilization comes first. Treat dehydration, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infection risk, then begin structured feeds using the facility-based SAM pathway with frequent small feeds and routine correction of potassium and magnesium deficits. (National Health Mission, 2013)
document_key_topics: Stabilization protocols for SAM in children
Diagnostic considerations for celiac disease and IBD
